title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
SEO Insertions on Does Group Insurance Cover Pre-Existing Diseases? - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Keywords: group insurance, pre-existing diseases, group insurance cover
Body Content:
It is a common misconception that insurance companies do not provide coverage for people with pre-existing diseases. This misconception prevents individuals from availing suitable insurance policies that may otherwise help them in times of healthcare requirements or emergencies. Nonetheless, when individuals buy private or individual policies, certain health insurance companies may not issue policies due to the factor of a pre-existing disease. However, when you speak of group insurance health plans, offered by employers to employees, these policies cover pre-existing diseases. Since an organisation is purchasing a policy, and there is a decreased risk of any fraud, pre-existing diseases are covered.
Generally, in case those who wish to buy policies suffer from pre-existing diseases, they have to undergo many procedures. Pre-existing conditions expose insurers to more financial risk. Therefore, they do not cover pre-existing medical conditions in individual policies. However, nowadays, you may find certain insurers who are willing to do so, but premium values may be very high for these. While buying a health insurance policy, you should be aware of the extent of its coverage, whether you have a pre-existing medical condition or not. If you are an employee of a company that offers health insurance as part of its group benefit plan, then it is equally important to find out the coverage scope of such a health insurance plan.
What is Classed as a Pre-existing Medical Condition for Health Insurance?
In case you are an employee of a company and are being offered group insurance by your employer, it is wise to find out the plan’s coverage scope. If you have a pre-existing medical condition, you may have to pay some extra fees associated with your plan. However, before you do this, it is vital to have some information about pre-existing medical issues and what they are.
A PED, or pre-existing condition, is an injury or medical condition that has existed in advance of the start date of an individual’s dental, disability, or health insurance policy.  Such a medical condition represents one which requires some ongoing medical treatment. Furthermore, treatment for the existing disease must have existed before the new plan goes into effect. These conditions are considered high-risk by health insurers, as the probability of raising claims/fraudulent claims is correspondingly high. Therefore, insurers may refuse to cover such individuals, or charge significantly higher premiums for the same.
In group insurance cover, the fact of pre-existing conditions does not arise as a company purchases a policy for a group of employees. In the eyes of the insurer, this entails less of a risk of fraudulent claims. The IRDAI has formulated new guidelines for PEDs with regard to the standardisation of terms of health insurance. As per the guidelines, these are the aspects to consider while classifying PEDs:
Any ailment/disease that has been diagnosed 48 months before the first health insurance policy is issued is classified as a pre-existing disease.
Any pre-existing diseases for which medical treatment was administered 48 months before the first policy issuance is also considered under the PED clause.
Any other ailment/condition, the symptoms of which result in a serious disease in the span of three months of a new policy’s issuance is also classified as a PED.
Typically, examples of pre-existing conditions are diabetes, cardiac problems, hypertension, etc. Individuals who opt for an individual health insurance plan have to pass a waiting period initially. This is usually between three and six months. Furthermore, insurers can opt not to renew a health insurance policy if a pre-existing condition is evidenced while the policy is valid.
In terms of group insurance, “pre-existing condition” represents an injury, ailment, or condition that an employee had prior to entering a contract with an employer. Hence, when an employee is initially part of a group insurance plan offered by a company, it may not have coverage for any ailment or disease present while the plan was applied for. Similarly, some group insurance policies may not even cover certain conditions you may have been afflicted with at any point in the past.
What Pre-existing Conditions are Covered in Group Health Insurance?
Group insurance cover is a kind of plan that gives benefits to a group of individuals under a single policy. The common pre-existing conditions covered in group health insurance are the following:
Diabetes
High Blood Pressure
Asthma
High Cholesterol
Conclusion
With the cost of health insurance ever-increasing, group health insurance offers employees a good way to get coverage. Premiums of individual policies can be high. Moreover, in the event of pre-existing conditions being present, individuals may not be eligible for certain private health insurance plans. In group insurance plans, as the company is purchasing insurance on behalf of employees, taken as a single unit, the question of pre-existing conditions does not hinder coverage. Such plans save employers tax and give staff more benefits than they may get if they opt for individual plans.
Sources:
